next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The notation in this section was denser than anything he'd encountered before. The patterns interlocked at scales his translational framework could barely hold — each decoded element revealing relationships to a dozen others, each of those connecting to a dozen more, until the structure he was building in his mind felt like trying to hold the shape of the entire castle at once when all you could manage was a single corridor.

He'd been at it for three hours. Or four. Time moved differently in the sub-collection — no windows, no change in the luminescent light from the walls, no sounds from the castle above. The stone rooms existed outside the rhythms of school life, and the longer he worked in them, the more that separation felt like a feature, not an accident. The sub-collection had not been designed for human scholars sitting on the floor with their legs going numb. If it had been designed at all, it had been designed for something that didn't need chairs.

The manuscript lay across his knees, its not-parchment surface faintly warm against his robes. Gavin shifted his weight and felt the cold of the flagstones through the thin fabric, sharp enough to remind him he had a body that was not especially comfortable. He'd brought a quill and scratch parchment for notes, but he hadn't written anything in over an hour. The notes he'd been taking were useless — approximations of approximations, English words circling concepts they couldn't hold.

The ward structure was the first thing the translation gave him. He'd known, from the section on the Builders, that wards e...
open_hooks_to_carry: The Builders must feel genuinely alien: beings for whom magic was not a tool but a medium of existence. Not wizards with better wands — something categorically different

The distinction between 'died' and 'left' must be precise. The Builders relocated, not through space but through a category of existence that the manuscript's notation can represent but that Gavin can only approximate
prev_tail_excerpt:
.... He lay in bed. He did not take the manuscript out from under his pillow, though he could feel it there, the slight warmth of the not-quite-parchment through the cotton pillowcase, and his hand — his right hand, the one that didn't shake — rested on the pillow next to it.

His left hand trembled against the blanket. The dormitory was dark. He could hear Duncan's breathing from the next bed over, too even, too controlled, the breathing of someone who was awake and managing it.

Miren was right about what she could see. She was right that he had checked out of the room he was standing in. She was right that his anger was pointed wrong. The only thing she was wrong about was the part she couldn't know — that the manuscript was not a distraction from what mattered but might, in a way he could not yet prove or articulate or even fully understand, be the thing that mattered most.

Or he was wrong. He was a fifteen-year-old Hufflepuff with mediocre marks in Ancient Runes and a shaking hand and a tendency to believe that the thing consuming his attention was the most important thing in the world, and maybe Miren was right about all of it, the visible and the invisible, and the manuscript was just a manuscript and he had checked out and the checking out was the whole story.

He didn't know. His hand shook. Duncan breathed. The manuscript waited under his pillow, warm and patient, and somewhere in its pages the passage about overlapping agreements held the shape of something he had almost understood before the world interrupted.

He lay in the dark and did not sleep for a long time.
state_deltas_required: The expanding scale of what the manuscript reveals — from 'interesting old magic' to 'there was a civilization that used magic as a medium of existence and they left Earth and they set wards around the planet before going'

Informational and tonal — the cosmic scope of the novel opens. Gavin understands that the First Art was created by beings for whom magic was as fundamental as air, that they departed Earth, and that they set planetary wards to protect what they left behind
